review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Okay so today was a lot and I'm still processing everything honestly. Got to the welcome center this morning and there was this family from Syria, father and two kids, and the little girl was maybe six years old and she had this tiny sketchbook she'd been carrying since they left Damascus. She showed me her drawings. Houses on fire. People running. A boat with stick figures packed so tight they looked like a fence. And then the last page was just colors—blue and orange and yellow—and she said that was what she wanted to see now. I nearly lost it right there but you can't do that in front of them so I just smiled and said her colors were beautiful.

Then I had to go straight from there to meet Pieter about the factory wall commission and he was talking about branding and "visual impact metrics" and I just sat there thinking about that little girl's sketchbook and how my murals are supposed to mean something but here I am discussing color palettes that will make people want to buy sustainable sneakers. Like what am I even doing? The sneakers are made ethically apparently, fair wages, recycled materials, so it's not like I'm selling my soul to Shell or whatever, but still. The whole meeting felt hollow.

Walked around the harbor for an hour after instead of taking the tram home. The water was that grey-green it gets in April and I kept thinking about the fishing boats in Lisbon and Maria who helped me there and how she said art that changes nothing is just decoration. Am I just decorating? The mural will be seen by thousands of people every day. Maybe one person thinks differently about what they consume. Maybe nobody does and it just looks cool and gets likes.

I don't know. I'm tired and I smell like spray paint and I can't stop seeing that little girl's drawings. Tomorrow I have to give Pieter an answer about the commission. I should probably say yes because I need the money but something in my chest is tight about it.

Nudge: "What would Maria say about the sneaker thing?"

Response: Ha. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it and that any wall is better than no wall. But then she'd also ask me what story I want to tell on it, and right now I don't have a story. I just have a brand deck with hex codes and a brief that says "inspire conscious consumption." Maybe that's the problem—it's their story, not mine.

I said no to Pieter. Told him yesterday and he was actually pretty understanding about it, said he'd keep me in mind for future projects, but I could tell he was disappointed. Whatever. I can't be responsible for everyone's disappointment.

What I didn't expect was how RELIEVED I felt after. Like I'd been holding my breath for a week and finally exhaled. Spent all of today at the welcome center instead of in my studio and it was exactly where I needed to be. We did an art session with some of the kids—just paper and crayons, nothing fancy—and I sat with Amira (the Syrian girl from before) and we drew together for two hours. She's so quiet but her hands never stop moving across the paper. She drew her grandmother today. A woman in a blue dress standing in a kitchen with yellow walls. She told me her grandmother makes the best knafeh but she doesn't know if she's still alive because they haven't been able to reach her since January.

I don't know how to hold that. I don't know what to do with the fact that I worried for a week about a sneaker mural while this kid doesn't know if her grandmother is alive. It makes everything I do feel small and pointless but also somehow more urgent? Like if I'm going to make things then they have to MATTER. They have to reach people the way Amira's drawings reach me.

I talked to Bram on the phone tonight—he's still in Berlin, been there four months now doing projections on the Reichstag for some climate group—and he said something that stuck with me. He said we can't measure ourselves by the size of the problem. That if everyone waited until they could fix everything, nothing would ever get made. I know he's right but it doesn't make the gap between what I want to do and what I can actually do feel any smaller.

Anyway. I'm going to start something new. Not sure what yet. Maybe something with the kids at the center, collaborative, their images mixed with mine. Something that tells THEIR story instead of a brand's story. No money in it probably but at this point I think I'd rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating.

Nudge: "What would the kids' piece look like?"

Response: I've been thinking about it all night actually. What if it wasn't a mural at all? What if it was something that moved? The boats in Lisbon worked because they existed in water, in motion—people saw them differently depending on the tide, the light. Amira's drawings are so full of movement even on flat paper. Houses falling, people running, boats tipping. What if we made something together that could actually move? Projections maybe, like what Bram does, but with their drawings animated somehow. I don't know how to do that technically but Bram might.

The more I think about it the more I realize the static murals were starting to feel like cages. I'd finish one and immediately want to be somewhere else, doing something else. Maybe that restlessness was telling me something—not just that I need to travel or whatever, but that the medium itself wasn't right anymore. I want to make things that breathe. That change. Things people can't just walk past and forget.

Took a half-day today because my girlfriend and I had reservations at this omakase place we have been wanting to try for months. My manager gave me this look when I submitted the leave request, like I was abandoning ship during a crisis. We are not even that busy right now. I completed everything on my plate before noon.

The meal was worth it. Twelve courses, and each one was like a small surprise. Jun and I barely talked during the first half because we were so focused on the food. By dessert, we were laughing about how serious we had been. Walking out, I felt genuinely recharged. My manager will survive. I needed that afternoon more than the company needed me at my desk pretending to look occupied.

Nudge: "You mentioned your manager's look and feeling like you needed that afternoon. What do you think drives that expectation to always be visibly busy, even when the work is done?"

Response: Honestly, I think it is just the culture here. A lot of the older staff measure commitment by hours, not output. They see leaving at 6pm as not caring, even if you finished everything. My manager came up that way, so he probably assumes everyone else should too. But I do not want to spend ten years proving myself by sitting around after my work is done. I would rather prove it by actually delivering, then having a life outside.

My dad asked me over dinner last night if I was taking my job seriously. I told him I just got a satisfactory rating on my performance review, and he said that was not the point. He meant whether I was staying late, whether I was sacrificing for the company the way he did at my age. I did not know how to explain to him that I work differently. I finished my projects ahead of deadline, I cover for my team when it matters, and I still make time for things I care about. That should count for something.

I spent today a bit distracted, replaying that conversation. Part of me wonders if he has a point, if I am missing something by not grinding harder. But then I think about my uncle, who worked sixty-hour weeks for thirty years and retired bitter and exhausted. I do not want that. I want to be good at my job and still have energy left for everything else.

Nudge: "You mentioned wondering if your dad has a point. What would it take for you to feel confident that your approach is the right one, without needing his validation?"

Response: I guess I would need to keep proving myself over time. If I can keep getting promoted and still leave at reasonable hours, that should speak for itself eventually. We will see.

Had my annual performance review with the principal today. She said I'm on track for contract renewal next August. That was the main thing I needed to hear. She mentioned some areas to improve—classroom management during transitions, parent communication frequency—but nothing that would jeopardize my position.

After the meeting, I reorganized my lesson plan binder for the rest of the semester. Having everything mapped out through December gives me peace of mind. I know exactly what materials I need, which units align with the standardized testing schedule, and when I can schedule parent conferences.

Miguel and I talked on the phone tonight. He got a small raise at the accounting firm. We updated our shared savings spreadsheet. At this rate, we'll hit our apartment down payment goal by next June. It feels good to see the numbers moving in the right direction.

[No nudge requested]

My colleague Sofia asked me again why I don't apply for that international teaching fellowship in Spain. She keeps saying it would be "an amazing experience" and that I'm "wasting my potential" staying here. I don't understand why she can't accept that I'm content with my situation.

Yes, the fellowship pays more. Yes, it would look impressive. But it's a two-year contract with no guarantee of renewal, in a country where I have no support system. What happens after those two years? I'd be starting over. Here, I have seniority building, pension contributions accumulating, and my mother nearby if anything goes wrong.

Sofia took a gap year after university and talks about it like everyone should do the same. Not everyone grew up with parents who could bail them out. Some of us had to figure things out early. My choices make sense for my life, even if they don't make sense to her.

[No nudge requested]

Stayed late grading papers. The empty classroom felt different tonight—quiet in a way that made me think. I wonder sometimes if I'll still be in this same room in ten years. The thought is comforting and unsettling at the same time. Maybe that contradiction means something.

Nudge: "What about that contradiction feels unsettling to you?"

Response: I think it's that I've built everything around wanting things to stay the same. But nothing actually stays the same forever, does it? Students graduate, teachers retire, policies change. Even the building gets renovated. Maybe I'm holding onto an idea of permanence that doesn't really exist. I'm not sure what to do with that realization yet.

The more I think about it the more I realize the static murals were starting to feel like cages. I'd finish one and immediately want to be somewhere else, doing something else. Maybe that restlessness was telling me something—not just that I need to travel or whatever, but that the medium itself wasn't right anymore. I want to make things that breathe. That change. Things people can't just walk past and forget.

My brother-in-law made a comment at Sunday dinner about how I must enjoy "having so much free time now" and I've been thinking about it all week. Free time? I coordinate three different after-school activities, manage the household budget down to the krone, and I've been teaching myself upholstery techniques that would take most people years to master. Just because there's no office title attached doesn't mean I'm sitting around watching television.

I know I shouldn't let it get to me. But there's this part of me that keeps wanting to list all the things I've done this month, all the skills I've picked up, all the ways I've improved how our home runs. The furniture business actually got two new orders last week. That's something, isn't it? Sometimes I feel like I'm running faster than I ever did at the firm, just with nobody keeping score anymore.

Nudge: "It sounds like that comment really stayed with you. What does "keeping score" mean to you now, outside of the traditional work context?"

Finished the oak sideboard restoration today. Client picked it up and actually teared up a bit when she saw it. Said it had been her grandmother's and she'd been looking for someone to bring it back properly for years. Took about forty hours total across two weeks.

Started sketching plans for a home workshop expansion. The garage layout isn't working anymore, especially with winter keeping me indoors more. Found some modular storage systems from a German supplier that could work. The twins helped me measure the space after school. Emma is surprisingly good at holding a tape measure steady. I'm thinking if I reorganize everything by March, I could take on bigger pieces. Maybe even start documenting the process to teach others eventually.

Nudge: "That's a meaningful project completion. What was most satisfying about the sideboard work - the technical challenge, the client's response, or something else?"

Response: Honestly? The technical part. Matching the original veneer grain pattern took me three tries and about five hours of research into 1920s Danish furniture styles. When I finally got it right, I took photos before and after just for my own records. The client's reaction was nice, genuinely touching even, but I knew it was good work before she saw it. The satisfaction came earlier, when I stepped back and could see I'd actually pulled it off. The workshop expansion isn't just about space. I want to be able to take on restoration challenges I can't even attempt right now with the current setup.

Lost the Grupo Modelo commission to Fernanda. They said they wanted "something bolder" for their new headquarters. I've done six projects for them over the years. My proposal fit their brand guidelines perfectly.

Nudge: "What does losing this one mean to you?"

Response: It's not about the money, though that hurts too. It's that I thought I knew what they wanted. I've spent years learning to give clients exactly what they ask for, and now that's not enough? Fernanda does these chaotic, aggressive pieces — nothing I would hang in my own home — and suddenly that's what serious companies want. I don't even know how to make work like that anymore. I'm not sure I ever did.

My mother called to tell me my cousin Rodrigo just got promoted to partner at his firm. She mentioned it three times during the conversation, which means she was making a point. Rodrigo and I used to be compared constantly growing up — he went the safe route, law school, the path my father would have chosen for me. When my paintings started selling, the comparisons stopped. Now I wonder if they'll start again.

The strange thing is I found myself defending my career to her even though she didn't attack it. I mentioned the board position at the collective, the new studio space, the clients I've worked with. I was listing credentials like she was interviewing me. My father would have been proud of the list. But sitting here after hanging up, I keep thinking about a canvas I stretched three months ago that's still blank in the corner. I bought the paint for it. I had an idea. I just haven't had time between commissions to work on anything that isn't already sold.

Okay so today was one of those days where everything just sort of piled up and I don't even know where to start really. Woke up early because Arjun had a nightmare, then couldn't go back to sleep because my mind started racing about the lunch I'm supposed to make for the pooja tomorrow. Mummyji has her specific expectations you know, the dal has to be a certain consistency, the rice has to be this particular grain she likes, and I keep thinking about all the relatives who will be there judging every single dish I put out.

But here's the thing that's been bothering me all day—Priya messaged the group chat about going to that new spa place next weekend. The one with the aromatherapy and the hot stone massage. And I just... I really want to go? Like my shoulders have been killing me for weeks, carrying Aanya around, sleeping in weird positions when she has colds. I haven't done anything just for myself in so long. The last time was probably Diwali when I bought that kurta and even then I felt guilty about spending on something that wasn't for the kids.

I typed out "I'm in!" three times and deleted it each time. Because what will everyone think? Mummyji will do that thing where she doesn't say anything but you can tell she's disappointed. She'll mention something about how she never had time for "all this spa-shpa" when she was raising three kids. And my husband will probably be fine with it but then I'll have to coordinate everything—who will give Arjun his evening milk, what if Aanya doesn't nap for anyone else, all of that. It just seems like so much effort for a few hours of relaxation that I'll probably spend feeling guilty anyway.

But also... don't I deserve to feel good? My body hurts. I'm tired all the time. My friends are going and they keep saying I never come to anything anymore. I miss laughing with them about nothing. I miss being Meera who had opinions and went places and wasn't just someone's mother and someone's wife and someone's daughter-in-law.

I don't know. I'll probably say no. It's easier.

Nudge: "You deleted that message three times—what were you afraid would happen?"

Response: That's... actually a good question. I think I was afraid they'd say yes. Does that make sense? If I had sent it and they said "great, come!" then I'd have to actually make a choice. Right now I can tell myself I never really wanted to go anyway, it was just a passing thought. But if I commit, then I have to face my mother-in-law's silence, have to justify wanting three hours for myself, have to look at my husband and ask for help instead of just handling everything quietly. It's easier to be the one who sacrifices. Everyone expects it. Nobody questions it. The moment I want something, suddenly I'm the difficult one.

Both kids have a stomach bug. I've been cleaning vomit since 3am. Lost count of how many sheet changes. Mummyji keeps hovering and suggesting home remedies that I know won't work but I just nod and say "yes, good idea" because I don't have the energy to argue. Husband took the morning off but had to go back for some urgent meeting so I've been solo since noon.

I keep thinking about that spa. Priya sent the confirmation today—they booked for Saturday. The message just sat there in my notifications while I was elbow-deep in laundry. I want to cry but even that feels like too much effort. All I want is to lie down somewhere quiet where nobody needs anything from me. Just for an hour. Is that too much to ask?

Nudge: "What went through your mind when you saw Priya's message?"

Response: Honestly? Jealousy. Burning jealousy. They're going to have mimosas and get massages and laugh and I'm here with sick children and sour laundry and my mother-in-law telling me for the fifth time that ajwain water fixes everything. I know it's not their fault. They don't have kids yet, their lives are different. But I just kept staring at that message thinking "that could have been me" and then immediately feeling guilty for even thinking that because my babies need me and I love them but god I'm so tired.

Kids are finally better. Mummyji made khichdi today without me asking—small gestures like this remind me she cares in her own way. Still thinking about the spa thing, wondering why it's so hard for me to just ask for what I want.

Nudge: "When's the last time you did ask?"

Response: I genuinely can't remember. Maybe when I was pregnant and asked for extra pillows? But even that felt like it was for the baby, not for me. Somewhere along the way I stopped asking. It was just easier to figure out how to not need things. Or to need smaller things that nobody would notice. Like my morning chai—I wake up twenty minutes early just so I can have that without anyone else's needs interrupting. That's my one thing. Twenty minutes of silence with a warm cup. Maybe that's enough. Maybe it isn't.

The end-of-year ceremony was today and I sat through three hours of speeches about commitment and building foundations and putting down roots. The headmaster went on about how teaching is about planting seeds and watching them grow over decades. Decades! I nearly laughed out loud. I've been here two years and already the walls feel like they're closing in. Same classroom, same view of the vineyard, same Tuesday staff meetings where Marie-Claire complains about the photocopier.

I know I'm supposed to feel grateful. The farmhouse is beautiful, the students are curious, the lavender fields smell like something from a perfume advertisement. But I keep thinking about that email from Kristina asking if I'd consider joining her school project in Kenya next spring. Teaching environmental science to kids who've never seen a glacier, helping build the curriculum from scratch. My heart raced just reading it.

Then I looked at my garden, the tomato plants I actually managed to keep alive this year, and felt this strange guilt. Like I'm betraying something by wanting to leave again. My daughter Elsa called last week and said, "Maman, don't you ever want to just... stay?" And I didn't know what to tell her because the honest answer is no. Not really. The staying is what makes me feel like I'm slowly turning to stone.

Nudge: "What is it about building something from scratch that gets to you?"

Booked the flight to Nairobi for a preliminary visit in August, told the school I'm taking a sabbatical to "explore professional development opportunities," which isn't exactly a lie. The tomatoes will be fine, Elsa will roll her eyes, and I'll deal with the farmhouse question later.

Nudge: "The farmhouse question?"

Response: It's just logistics. Whether to rent it out or have someone check on it while I'm gone. Nothing dramatic.

My brother-in-law made a comment at Sunday dinner about how I must enjoy "having so much free time now" and I've been thinking about it all week. Free time? I coordinate three different after-school activities, manage the household budget down to the krone, and I've been teaching myself upholstery techniques that would take most people years to master. Just because there's no office title attached doesn't mean I'm sitting around watching television.

I know I shouldn't let it get to me. I want to be able to take on restoration challenges I can't even attempt right now with the current setup.

Good class today. The students actually debated about the new coastal development project instead of just waiting for me to give them the "right" answer. These moments remind me why I chose this path.

Nudge: "What made them engage differently today?"

Response: I think they're just more comfortable with the format now. We've been doing discussions for a few weeks.

Long day. Parent-teacher meetings are draining in a way that teaching never is. One parent spent twenty minutes explaining why her son shouldn't have to learn about "foreign problems" like ocean pollution. I tried to explain that the Arabian Sea is not foreign to Kerala, but I could see her eyes glazing over.

Then the principal pulled me aside afterward to suggest I "tone down the activism" in my lessons. Said some parents have been complaining. I nodded and said I'd think about it. Not sure what I'm supposed to think about, honestly.

Nudge: "What did that nod cost you?"

Response: Nothing. It's just politics. Part of working in any institution.

Spent the weekend thinking about the principal's comment. I talked it over with Arjun and he made a good point - I can either fight every battle loudly and burn out, or I can be strategic. Pick what matters most and find ways to teach it that don't raise red flags.

I don't love the idea of softening my approach. But I also know that if I get pushed out, whoever replaces me will probably just teach straight from the textbook. At least I'm here. At least these kids are asking questions about their own coastline. That has to count for something.
